fields.Text:
VCUG with ureterocele in child with recurrent UTIs. Associated diagnosis?

{{c1::Ureteral duplication}}
fields.Extra:
Q4. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Ureterocele appears as a filling defect in the bladder during early filling phase on anteroposterior images. Oblique projections clearly demonstrate ureteroceles with or without eversion. Simple intravesical ureteroceles appear as smooth filling defects within the bladder.

fields.Text:
Budd-Chiari patient with arterial enhancing liver mass and washout. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Hepatocellular carcinoma}}
fields.Extra:
Q5. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Hepatocellular carcinoma in Budd-Chiari appears as a solitary mass with arterial hyperenhancement and venous washout, but regenerative nodules can frequently mimic this pattern. Hepatobiliary phase magnetic resonance imaging can be used to distinguish malignancy from benign nodules, showing hypointensity and elevated alpha-fetoprotein in cases of HCC.





Open review Q5

fields.Text: In Budd-Chiari, HCC appears as {{c1::arterial hyperenhancing mass with venous washout and hepatobiliary phase hypointensity/elevated AFP}}.

Vital Concept: Hepatocellular carcinoma in Budd-Chiari appears as a solitary mass with arterial hyperenhancement and venous washout, but regenerative nodules can frequently mimic this pattern. Hepatobiliary phase magnetic resonance imaging can be used to distinguish malignancy from benign nodules, showing hypointensity and elevated alpha-fetoprotein in cases of HCC.

fields.Text:
3-year-old with palpable flank mass and heterogeneous renal mass. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Wilms tumor}}
fields.Extra:
Q9. Incorrect; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Wilms tumor is the most common renal tumor in children with a mean age of diagnosis at 3 to 4 years. On CT, it appears as a heterogeneous mass showing less enhancement than surrounding normal renal parenchyma. Always assess the renal vein and inferior vena cava for tumor thrombus. Most frequently metastasizes to the lungs.

The cortex appears abnormally thickened with an irregular cortical-white matter junction. Small fine undulating gyri may be difficult to detect on imaging.

fields.Text:
Uterine artery embolization. Absolute contraindication?

{{c1::Current pelvic inflammatory disease}}
fields.Extra:
Q16. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Active pelvic inflammatory disease is an absolute contraindication to uterine artery embolization. UAE causes fibroid necrosis which creates necrotic tissue that serves as a nidus for bacterial proliferation. Pre-existing pelvic infection combined with necrotic tissue significantly increases sepsis risk and life-threatening complications.

fields.Text:
Scrotal US with large cystic epididymal lesion and floating internal echoes. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Spermatocele}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q18. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Large thin-walled cystic structure with innumerable small floating internal echoes is most compatible with spermatocele, representing retained spermatozoa in a dilated epididymal duct. The internal echoes distinguish it from simple epididymal cysts and confirm the benign nature, allowing conservative management in asymptomatic patients.

fields.Text: A spermatocele is a cystic epididymal lesion with {{c1::innumerable low-level/floating internal echoes from spermatozoa}}.

fields.Text:
Headache with peg-like tonsillar descent below foramen magnum. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Chiari I malformation}}
fields.Extra:
Q19. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Chiari 1 shows cerebellar tonsillar herniation below the foramen magnum greater than 3mm in children or 5mm in adults. Tonsils appear peg-like with effacement of CSF spaces around the foramen magnum. Associated syringomyelia is common. Brainstem position remains normal, distinguishing it from Chiari 1.5 malformation.

fields.Text: Chiari I malformation is tonsillar herniation below the foramen magnum, typically {{c1::>5 mm in adults or >3 mm in children}}.

fields.Text: Radiation protection uses the {{c1::linear no-threshold}} dose-response model.
fields.Extra:
Q25. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The Linear No-Threshold model assumes any radiation dose, no matter how small, carries some cancer risk with no safe threshold, and that cancer risk increases linearly with dose. This conservative model forms the basis for radiation protection standards in medical imaging and occupational exposure limits.

{{c1::Functional gallbladder disorder}}

Normal GBEF effectively excludes symptomatic gallbladder disease.

fields.Text: Doppler ultrasound beam intensities are typically about {{c1::50 times higher than B-mode}}.
fields.Extra:
Q30. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Ultrasound intensity increases with information complexity demands: B-mode (tissue structure) requires lowest power, Color Doppler (flow detection) needs moderate power, and Spectral Doppler (detailed velocity analysis) demands highest power, directly correlating with thermal bioeffect potential.




Open review Q30

fields.Text: Large contrast extravasation with concerning symptoms should prompt {{c1::surgical consultation}}.
fields.Extra:
Q31. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Contrast media extravasation (CMEV) refers to the leakage of intravenously administered contrast media from the normal intravascular compartment into surrounding soft tissues. It is a well-known complication of contrast-enhanced CT scanning. Large volumes (>50 mL) of high-osmolar contrast media are known to induce significant tissue damage including skin ulceration, soft-tissue necrosis, and compartment syndrome, although this is rare.




Open review Q31

fields.Text:
Stable benign axillary/breast lesion in male patient. BI-RADS?

{{c1::BI-RADS 2}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q32. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: BI-RADS 2 is a benign category in breast imaging reporting and data system. A finding placed in this category should have a 100% chance of being benign. Examples of such lesions or findings include: calcified fibroadenomas, multiple secretory calcifications, fat-containing lesions (oil cysts, breast lipomas, galactoceles, mixed density hamartomas), cutaneous neurofibromas, intramammary lymph nodes, epidermal inclusion cysts, simple breast cysts, breast implants.

fields.Text:
RUQ pain, leukocytosis, positive Murphy sign, gallbladder wall >3 mm. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Acute cholecystitis}}
fields.Extra:
Q33. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Normal gallbladder wall thickness is less than 3mm. Murphy's sign is a test performed for cholelithiasis. A positive sign is if pain occurs when the inflamed gallbladder is palpated by the examiner. It is performed by palpating the right subcostal area while the patient is asked to take in and hold a deep breath.

fields.Text:
FOOSH/hockey wrist pain with nondisplaced scaphoid fracture. Initial treatment?

{{c1::Cast immobilization}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q39. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Scaphoid fracture is the most common carpal bone fracture. Patients will typically present with pain around the dorsal wrist and/or the anatomical snuffbox after a fall on an outstretched hand. In case of nondisplaced/minimally displaced (<1 mm) fracture, cast immobilization is the best initial treatment with union rate of approximately 90%.

Q40. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: The mandibular nerve (third division of the trigeminal nerve V3) passes through the foramen ovale.

fields.Text: Gamma camera sensitivity is increased by collimators with {{c1::larger holes and shorter septa}}.
fields.Extra:
Q41. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Gamma camera sensitivity depends on collimator design - collimators with larger holes and shorter septa provide higher sensitivity but worse spatial resolution.

fields.Text:
Fever with neck/back pain and disc/endplate infection on MRI. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Spondylodiscitis}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q42. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Spondylodiscitis is an infection of the intervertebral disc space and adjacent vertebral bodies. Spondylodiscitis can be complicated by epidural phlegmon/abscess.

fields.Text: Spondylodiscitis is infection of the {{c1::disc space and adjacent vertebral bodies}}.
fields.Extra:

fields.Text:
Bilateral thalamic venous infarct pattern. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Deep cerebral venous thrombosis}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q43. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Deep cerebral venous thrombosis causes profound neurological deterioration and coma from bilateral thalamic involvement, affecting internal cerebral veins and vein of Galen.

fields.Text: Deep cerebral venous thrombosis involves the {{c1::internal cerebral veins/vein of Galen}} and can cause bilateral thalamic injury.

{{c1::Surgical consultation}}

fields.Text:
Unconscious drowning patient with diffuse cortical/deep gray restricted diffusion. Etiology?

{{c1::Hypoxic ischemic encephalopathy}}
fields.Extra:
Q45. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy in adults typically shows diffuse cortical and deep grey matter restricted diffusion with uniformly poor prognosis. Only watershed infarcts and isolated basal ganglia involvement without cortical lesions have relatively preserved outcomes.




Open review Q45

fields.Text: Adult hypoxic ischemic encephalopathy often shows {{c1::diffuse cortical and deep gray matter restricted diffusion}}.
fields.Extra:

Only watershed infarcts and isolated basal ganglia involvement without cortical lesions have relatively preserved outcomes.

fields.Text:
Floor-of-mouth cystic lesion. Etiology?

{{c1::Sublingual/minor salivary gland ranula}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q46. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Ranulas arise from the sublingual or minor salivary glands. They are considered plunging if they extend to the submandibular space.




Open review Q46

fields.Text: Ranulas arise from {{c1::sublingual or minor salivary glands}}.
fields.Extra:

fields.Text: Normal cervical length is at least about {{c1::30 mm}}.
fields.Extra:
Q47. Correct; difficulty: hard. Cervical length is the distance between the internal and the external cervical os, as measured with electronic calipers.

fields.Text:
Progressive jaundice with hilar extrahepatic duct occlusion. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Klatskin tumor}}
fields.Extra:
Q48. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography demonstrates marked intrahepatic biliary duct dilatation (green arrows) without opacification of the extrahepatic ducts, and irregularly-shaped extrahepatic duct occlusion (blue arrow).

fields.Text: Klatskin tumor is a {{c1::hilar cholangiocarcinoma}} causing intrahepatic duct dilatation with extrahepatic duct obstruction.

fields.Extra:
Q49. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Knowing the dosimetry for commonly performed radiologic exams is important for radiation safety and patient care.

fields.Text:
Smoker with enhancing mass invading LV apex. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Metastatic lung carcinoma}}
fields.Extra:
Q50. Correct; difficulty: easy. The irregularly enhancing mass (blue arrows) invading the left ventricular (green arrow) apex is most concerning for malignancy involving the myocardium.





Open review Q50

fields.Text: A lung cancer patient with myocardial mass invading the LV apex most likely has {{c1::cardiac metastasis from lung carcinoma}}.
fields.Extra:
Q50. Correct; difficulty: easy. The irregularly enhancing mass (blue arrows) invading the left ventricular (green arrow) apex is most concerning for malignancy involving the myocardium.






Open review Q50

fields.Text:
Transplant patient with fever/headache and ring-enhancing restricted-diffusion lesion. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Intracerebral abscess}}
fields.Extra:
Q51. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Intracerebral abscess demonstrates smooth rim enhancement with a characteristic T2 hypointense capsule surrounding central T2 hyperintense pus. Restricted diffusion within the cavity is the key distinguishing feature from cystic neoplasms.




Open review Q51

fields.Text: Intracerebral abscess is distinguished by {{c1::smooth rim enhancement, T2 hypointense capsule, and central restricted diffusion}}.
fields.Extra:
Q51. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Intracerebral abscess demonstrates smooth rim enhancement with a characteristic T2 hypointense capsule surrounding central T2 hyperintense pus. Restricted diffusion within the cavity is the key distinguishing feature from cystic neoplasms.

fields.Text:
Gas-filled detector curve. Region where ionization chambers function?

{{c1::Region B}}
fields.Extra:
Q53. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Gas-filled detector operating regions: recombination region (A) where voltage is too low for complete ion collection, ionization chamber (B) for dose measurements, proportional region (C) for spectroscopy with gas amplification, Geiger-Müller region (D) for maximum sensitivity without energy discrimination, and continuous discharge region (E) where detector malfunctions.




Open review Q53

fields.Text: Ionization chambers operate in gas-filled detector region {{c1::B}}.
fields.Extra:
Q53. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Gas-filled detector operating regions: recombination region (A) where voltage is too low for complete ion collection, ionization chamber (B) for dose measurements, proportional region (C) for spectroscopy with gas amplification, Geiger-Müller region (D) for maximum sensitivity without energy discrimination, and continuous discharge region (E) where detector malfunctions.





Open review Q53

fields.Text: Ionization chambers function in gas-filled detector region {{c1::B}}, not the Geiger-Muller region.

Vital Concept: Gas-filled detector operating regions: recombination region (A) where voltage is too low for complete ion collection, ionization chamber (B) for dose measurements, proportional region (C) for spectroscopy with gas amplification, Geiger-Müller region (D) for maximum sensitivity without energy discrimination, and continuous discharge region (E) where detector malfunctions.

fields.Text: Annual public radiation dose limit is {{c1::100 mrem / 1 mSv}}.
fields.Extra:
Q54. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The radiation dose limit to a member of the general public is 1 mSv or 100 mrem (0.02 mSv/ hr or 2 mrem/hr) The annual dose limit for members of the general public from licensed operations is 100 mrem (1 mSv), excluding background radiation and medical procedures they personally receive.

Postpartum bleeding 4 weeks after delivery. Endometrial finding suggesting endometritis?

{{c1::Intrauterine air}}
fields.Extra:
Q55. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Explanation: The presence of intrauterine air more than 3 weeks after delivery is suggestive of infection.
